flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of the throw keyword in Java?
flashcards.back: The primary purpose of the throw keyword is to signal that an exceptional condition has occurred in the code, allowing for deliberate triggering of exception handling.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax for throwing an exception using the throw statement in Java?
What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
flashcards.back: Checked exceptions are subclasses of the Exception class (excluding RuntimeException and its subclasses) and are checked at compile-time, while unchecked exceptions are subclasses of the RuntimeException class or the Error class and are not checked at compile-time.

flashcards.front: How does the throw keyword differ from the throws keyword in Java?
flashcards.back: The throw keyword is used to explicitly throw an exception, while the throws keyword is used to declare exceptions that a method may throw.

flashcards.front: What happens to the code after a throw statement is executed in Java?
flashcards.back: The code after a throw statement is executed is unreachable and will result in a compile-time error.

flashcards.front: Can custom exceptions be thrown using the throw keyword in Java?
flashcards.back: Yes, custom exceptions can be thrown by extending the Exception class or one of its subclasses.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of including an error message when throwing an exception in Java?
flashcards.back: The purpose of including an error message is to provide more informative error messages and to help with debugging.

flashcards.front: What type of object must be thrown using the throw keyword in Java?
flashcards.back: A Throwable object or its subclasses must be thrown using the throw keyword.
